title: The OSI Model and TCP/IP
description:
## How Networking Is Layered

The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model** is a conceptual framework that splits network communication into **7 layers**. Each layer provides services to the one above it and relies on the one below. The model is a teaching and troubleshooting tool — real implementations don't map perfectly to it, but the vocabulary ("that's a Layer 4 load balancer", "a Layer 7 proxy") is everywhere in DevOps.

### The 7 layers (top to bottom)

| # | Layer | Responsibility | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | **Application** | Interfaces apps use to access the network | HTTP, DNS, SSH |
| 6 | **Presentation** | Encoding, encryption, compression | TLS, JPEG, ASCII/UTF-8 |
| 5 | **Session** | Establishing/maintaining/closing sessions | Session setup, RPC |
| 4 | **Transport** | End-to-end delivery, ports, reliability | TCP, UDP |
| 3 | **Network** | Logical addressing and routing between networks | IP, ICMP, routers |
| 2 | **Data Link** | Framing and delivery on the local link; MAC addresses | Ethernet, switches, ARP |
| 1 | **Physical** | Transmitting raw bits over a medium | Cables, fiber, radio, NICs |

> Mnemonic (top → bottom): **A**ll **P**eople **S**eem **T**o **N**eed **D**ata **P**rocessing.

### Encapsulation

As data travels **down** the stack on the sender, each layer wraps it with its own header (and the link layer adds a trailer). On the receiver it travels **up**, each layer stripping its header:

```
[L7 data] -> [L4 TCP hdr | data] -> [L3 IP hdr | ...] -> [L2 frame | ... | trailer] -> bits
```

### How TCP/IP maps onto OSI

The **TCP/IP model** (what the Internet actually runs on) is simpler — 4 layers — and collapses several OSI layers together:

| TCP/IP layer | Covers OSI layers | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Application** | 7 + 6 + 5 | HTTP, DNS, TLS, SSH |
| **Transport** | 4 | TCP, UDP |
| **Internet** | 3 | IP, ICMP |
| **Link / Network Access** | 2 + 1 | Ethernet, Wi-Fi, ARP |

### Why it matters

- A **Layer 4** load balancer routes by IP/port without reading the payload; a **Layer 7** proxy can route by URL path or HTTP host.
- When debugging, the OSI layers give you a checklist: cable/link up (L1/L2)? routable IP (L3)? port open (L4)? right app protocol/cert (L6/L7)?
